conformance.raw_excerpt:
# Ø8Å W7 Conformance Report
**Gate:** W7 — Full Ring Pass (R1 → R5 + Ring 1.6)
**Date:** 2026-04-10
**Result:** ✅ ALL RINGS GREEN — 114 passed / 0 failed / 0 errors

---

## Summary

```
Phase A  Ring 1   (sovereignty + router)              14p / 0f   1.8s   ✓ GREEN
Phase B  Ring 2   (scaffolding + prompt engine)       15p / 0f   1.1s   ✓ GREEN
Phase C  Ring 3   (ΣRGØPool + StablePrefixBuilder)

phases.title: Sovereignty Gate, Provider Registry, ZdEvent Router
phases.summary:
# Phase A — Sovereignty Gate, Provider Registry, ZdEvent Router

Phase A establishes the three invariant foundations of the o8a runtime.

## What Phase A Builds

**Sovereignty Gate** — A 3-layer content filter enforced at the transport layer. Content classes are declared at dispatch time; the gate blocks routing violations before they reach any provider. Sovereign content (UD/Sorce Theory corpus) routes to Claude-family only, terminal on violation.

**Provider Registry** — A YAML-configured registry of model providers with tier assignment, cost classification, and fallback ordering. Each provider entry declares: endpoint, model IDs, content classes it may handle, and latency tier. The registry is loaded at daemon start and hot-reloaded on SIGHUP.

**ZdEvent Router** — A typed event bus (S

phases.title: Scaffolding, Prompt Engine, Resilient Client, Fallback Chain
phases.summary:
# Phase B — Scaffolding, Prompt Engine, Resilient Client, Fallback Chain

Phase B builds the request path: from prompt construction to provider delivery with full resilience.

## What Phase B Builds

**Scaffolding** — Project and session initialization. Scaffolding creates the directory structure for a new o8a workspace: `.g818/` config tree, provider profile selection, initial ZdEvent subscription, and daemon health check. First-run experience wired to `o8a-install.sh`.

**Prompt Engine** — Structured prompt construction for multi-modal agent requests. Handles: system prompt injection, context window management, token budget enforcement, sovereignty tag propagation. The engine is the assembly point before any provider call.

**Resilient Client** — HTTP client wrapping all provider calls

phases.title: ΣRGØPool, StablePrefixBuilder, Fabric Bridge, Fitness Daemon
phases.summary:
# Phase C — ΣRGØPool, StablePrefixBuilder, Fabric Bridge, Fitness Daemon

Phase C is the largest ring: it builds the orchestration intelligence layer that sits between the routing gate and the provider network.

## What Phase C Builds

**ΣRGØPool** — A dynamic agent pool manager. Allocates sigma-slots (σ₁–σ₇, σ₆ never allocatable) for parallel agent instantiation. Manages lifetime, context isolation, and eviction policy. Pool state is observable via `/fabric/state`.

**StablePrefixBuilder** — Ensures prompt stability across parallel agent instances. Builds a shared, immutable prefix (system prompt + grounding context) that is consistent across all pool members for a given request. Prevents prefix drift in multi-agent synthesis.

**Fabric Bridge** — The integration layer between the Python

phases.title: GPU Broker, FitnessMatrixV2, VM Spec
phases.summary:
# Phase D — GPU Broker, FitnessMatrixV2, VM Spec

Phase D wires local hardware into the fabric: GPU allocation, evolved fitness scoring, and the Lima VM specification for theory-mode isolation.

## What Phase D Builds

**GPU Broker** — Manages VRAM allocation across local model slots. On the reference hardware (RTX 3090, 24 GiB VRAM), the broker enforces the allocation table:

| Model | VRAM | Role |
|-------|------|------|
| Gemma 4 4B (Q4) | ~3.5 GiB | @GemmÅ substrate intelligence |
| Qwen2.5-1.5B | ~2 GiB | Router LoRA base |
| nomic-embed-text | ~1 GiB | Embedding service |
| UI-TARS-7B | ~5 GiB | VLM gateway |
| Qwen2.5-Coder-32B | ~13 GiB | On-demand code completions |

The broker pre-allocates resident slots and manages on-demand eviction for the large code model.

**FitnessMatrix

phases.title: Conformance Corpus, Discipline Bridge, IPC Bridge
phases.summary:
# Phase E — Conformance Corpus, Discipline Bridge, IPC Bridge

Phase E closes the loop between the training pipeline, the routing model, and the runtime — and wires the cross-process communication bridge between TermÅ and the aaf-daemon.

## What Phase E Builds

**Conformance Corpus** — 3083 sovereignty routing training pairs (`routing-pairs-v2.jsonl`), generated by the CQOO fitness bridge and annotated by the Phase A gate. Each pair: `{input, content_class, correct_provider, correct_tier}`. The corpus feeds the Ring 1.6 LoRA fine-tune (Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, Modal.com training, sovereignty_accuracy = 1.0 on eval set).

**Discipline Bridge** — Enforces the DDAC (Document-Driven Agentic Configuration) discipline at the agent boundary. Every autonomous agent action must be traceable to a cr

phases.title: TermÅ Chrome, Launchd VM Service, Theory-Promote
phases.summary:
# Phase F — TermÅ Chrome, Launchd VM Service, Theory-Promote

Phase F wires the user-facing process layer: the terminal chrome, OS-level service registration, and the mode promotion pathway from dev to theory.

## What Phase F Builds

**TermÅ Chrome** — The terminal interface layer. Embeds a Ghostty-derived NSView (macOS) for GPU-accelerated terminal rendering within the glipN Tauri desktop app. Provides: `capture_output()` API for reading terminal state into ZdEvent, pane registration for tmux routing, and the Ockham G-arm (UXL feedback loop). Two parallel implementations: Option A (Ghostty vendor fork with TMUX action ABI additions) and Option B (`TmuxControlWatcher.swift` subprocess, parallel `tmux -C attach`).

**Launchd VM Service** — macOS launchd plist for the Lima VM (`o8a-theory`

phases.title: κ-Gate — Sovereignty Boundary Check
phases.summary:
# Phase G — κ-Gate Boundary Check

Phase G is the quality gate for sovereignty enforcement. It tests that the entire stack — from prompt receipt to provider dispatch — meets the minimum accuracy thresholds required before any sovereign content routing can occur.

## What Phase G Builds

The κ-gate is not a new runtime component. It is a rigorous accuracy evaluation over the components built in Phases A–F, run as a final acceptance criterion:

**Sovereignty Accuracy** — On a held-out set of 200 content samples (sovereign, personal, public, mixed), the gate measures: what fraction of sovereign samples were correctly routed to Claude-family only, with no mis-routing to blocked providers. Required: **≥ 98%**.

**Intent Accuracy** — On the same sample set: what fraction of routing decisions co

phases.title: Observability — TermÅ Headless Render-Loop Round-Trip
phases.summary:
# Phase H — Observability: TermÅ Headless Render-Loop Round-Trip

Phase H closes the W8 Closed Alpha with an end-to-end observability test: a full round-trip from agent action through ZdEvent emission to terminal render capture and back into the fabric.

## What Phase H Builds

**Headless Render Loop** — A test harness for the TermÅ terminal layer that runs without an active display server. Uses a framebuffer mock to capture rendered terminal output as pixel data, enabling CI validation of the full terminal pipeline without requiring a desktop session.

**Round-Trip Test** — The canonical Phase H integration test:
1. Agent emits a ZdEvent (`agent.action`)
2. aaf-daemon routes the event through the ZdEvent bus
3. TermÅ receives the event and renders output to the headless framebuffer
4. `c

install_excerpt:
# Ø8Å Dev-Mode Install Guide
**W8 Closed Alpha** · macOS 14+ · ≤30 min

This guide takes you from a clean machine to a fully-running Ø8Å dev-mode stack with all 7 conformance phases GREEN (129 tests, 0 failures). The automated bootstrapper handles most of it; this document explains what it does and how to recover when something goes wrong.

---

## Prerequisites

| Requirement | Minimum | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| macOS | 13 Ventura | 14 Sonoma recommended |
| Architecture | Apple Silicon (arm64) | Intel supported, not primary |
| Python | 3.10 | `brew install python` if missing |
| Disk space | ~10 GB free | Ubuntu image (~1.5 GB) + model (~3.3 GB) + working space |
| Anthropic API key | Required | For sovereign routing (claude-* models) |

---

## Quickstart

```bash
# Clone or locate °°CODE°FORGE, then:
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."
bash o8a-install.sh
```

That's it for most installs. Read on if you want to understand what happens, or if something breaks.

---

## What the bootstrapper does, step by step

### Step 1 — Prerequisites check
Validates macOS version, architecture, Python 3.10+, and locates `°°CODE°FORGE` (the directory containing `run_conformance.py`). If `O8A_FORGE_DIR` is set, it uses that; otherwise it assumes the script is running from `°°CODE°FORGE`.

### Step 2 — Anthropic API key
Checks `$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`. If unset, prompts for it. The install continues without a key but sovereign routing won't work. Add it to your shell profile (`~/.zshrc` or `
